claim: Agents should only be given access to two of three capabilities: file access, internet access, or code execution. Combining file access with code execution without internet isolation is a security vulnerability.
actionable: When designing agent systems, explicitly restrict agent capabilities to maximum two of: filesystem access, internet access, code execution. Run agents with internet access in isolated VMs without filesystem access to reduce attack surface.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: NVIDIA's Dynamo is a data center scale inference engine that separates prefill and decode phases into disaggregated pipelines, allowing independent scaling and different GPU allocation strategies.
actionable: Use Dynamo's prefill/decode disaggregation to run compute-bound prefill on high-throughput GPUs and memory-bound decode on memory-optimized hardware, achieving better cost/latency tradeoffs than single-replica scaling.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Prefill and decode have fundamentally different computational profiles: prefill is compute-bound (uses quadratic KV cache operations), while decode is memory-bound (retrieves linear memory, does linear compute per token).
actionable: When optimizing inference, size GPU clusters for prefill with high compute density and for decode with high memory bandwidth. This explains why disaggregation can improve overall throughput.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: NVLink provides ~500 GB/s unidirectional bandwidth between H100 GPUs within an MBLink domain (8 GPU max), while Infiniband drops to ~50 GB/s—an order of magnitude difference—forcing multi-node systems to sacrifice intra-node communication speed.
actionable: When scaling inference across multiple nodes, minimize cross-node communication by aligning model sharding strategy to MBLink domain boundaries (up to 8 H100s), or redesign the computation to tolerate slower Infiniband links.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: NVIDIA's SOL principle demands first-principles analysis of speed/latency constraints: define the theoretical maximum speed (like speed of light), then work backward to understand actual bottlenecks rather than accepting constraints at face value.
actionable: When told a task will take X time, ask: what is the absolute physical/algorithmic limit? Could this be done in a day if we did it manually once? This identifies real constraints vs. organizational friction.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Inference optimization requires balancing three axes: quality (accuracy of results), cost (operational expense), and latency (response time). No single configuration optimizes all three; Dynamo provides knobs to explore the Pareto frontier.
actionable: When deploying inference at scale, explicitly define your SLA (latency requirement), choose the model size that meets quality needs, then solve for minimum cost. Use Dynamo or similar tools to experiment across tensor parallelism, batch sizes, and quantization.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Hardware-model co-design is critical for long context: models like Deepseek V3 use Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) to reduce KV cache footprint (128k context fits in 8GB vs. 40-80GB for Llama 405B), proving architecture choices must match hardware constraints.
actionable: When choosing or training a long-context model, examine its attention architecture (MLA, GQA, etc.), not just context length claims. Test actual KV cache size on your hardware to validate claims.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Grove, Dynamo's Kubernetes orchestration component, allows dynamic ratio changes between prefill and decode workers as workload characteristics shift (e.g., input length spikes). Leader-Worker Set cannot represent this pairing dynamically.
actionable: Use Grove/Dynamo for multi-node inference with variable prefill/decode ratios. If stuck with Leader-Worker Set, pre-configure separate replicas for prefill-heavy and decode-heavy workloads rather than a fixed ratio.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Test-time scaling (retrying requests, reasoning, tool calls, agents) is becoming mainstream as models allow variable compute at inference time, enabling better results without retraining.
actionable: For critical tasks, allow agents/inference systems multiple retry attempts with query context from failures. Combine with 'test again' guidance: recent Google research shows re-prompting with failed context often succeeds.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: CLI tools are better for LLM agents than APIs because: (1) pre-training data is heavily skewed toward CLI/shell documentation, (2) command discovery is deterministic (discoverable via --help), (3) bash is ubiquitous and avoids environment version conflicts.
actionable: When building tools for agents, prioritize CLI interfaces with clear --help and examples. Agents will more reliably invoke CLIs than dynamically-constructed API calls, especially on multi-tool tasks.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Agents trained for specific tool harnesses (e.g., Claude Code with its context-structuring framework) achieve higher performance than generic agents, but this couples the agent to that harness—reusing agents requires retraining or post-training.
actionable: If deploying a specialized agent tool, either: (a) train agents specifically for that tool's harness, or (b) invest in post-training to adapt agents to new tools. Generic agents will underperform specialized ones.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Momentum beats hierarchy at NVIDIA: if you can show working prototypes and make progress, you can push forward without explicit approval. Email-based mosh pits (large CC lists) allow ideas to surface and be adopted by influential people.
actionable: In large orgs, build a working proof-of-concept, then email influential people (~50-100 person threads) showing results. Momentum + working code > formal proposals.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Coding agents outperform general-purpose agents because they have full access to the terminal, allowing them to write, compile, test, and debug code in a tight loop—each error feeds back immediately into the agent.
actionable: To improve agent performance, give it full terminal access and clear build/test commands. Coding agents thrive on immediate feedback loops; general agents fail without this tight coupling.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Current production agents (Claude Code, Cursor) run for 20-45 minutes per task; experimental setups can exceed this by specifying 'keep going' prompts, but longer runs show diminishing returns on optimization before future efficiency gains enable longer runs.
actionable: Plan agent deployment for 20-45 minute SLAs. For longer research tasks, use sub-agent delegation rather than single long-running agents; this reduces token waste and improves observability.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: NVIDIA's build.nvidia.com inference API allows free (rate-limited) access to latest open-source models with 24-hour deployment SLAs, making it ideal for rapid prototyping and hacker projects.
actionable: For MVP inference workloads, use build.nvidia.com instead of setting up your own Dynamo cluster. It handles model ops and updates automatically.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Brev simplifies SSH/GPU provisioning by reducing friction to a single interface: instead of multi-page cloud forms with hidden dropdowns, Brev shows available GPUs as large, animated chips and handles SSH setup in one click.
actionable: Use Brev for quick GPU provisioning in development. Its 'big chip' UX is intentional DX—it reduces cognitive load vs. traditional cloud dashboards.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Model serving requires explicit decisions on how to split models across hardware: tensor parallelism (scale-up), pipeline parallelism, and specialist routing. Everyone solves this differently; there is no 'recipe'—it requires domain knowledge and iteration.
actionable: Do not expect fine-tuning or serving to have standardized recipes. Prototype multiple parallelism strategies (tensor parallel size, batch size, quantization) and measure on your hardware—the optimal config is deployment-specific.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Long-context limits are fundamental to architecture, not just sequence length claims: scaling laws for attention are not linear, and even with optimizations like MLA, hidden limits exist (e.g., 1M tokens capped for 2+ years).
actionable: Do not assume published context lengths are practical limits. Benchmark actual KV cache memory and latency on your hardware. Expect architectural breakthroughs ('unhoblers') to enable the next 10x jump.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup

claim: Sub-agent architectures (main agent delegates to specialized sub-agents with limited prompts) reduce token waste and improve observability vs. single long-running agents.
actionable: For complex tasks, design agents with sub-agent delegation: a main router agent decides which specialized sub-agent to invoke (e.g., search agent, code agent, summarization agent). This improves interpretability and cost.
source_title: Agent Inference at the "Speed of Light" — How NVIDIA moves like a $4.3 Trillion Startup
